subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical development with a recognisable introductionâbodyâconclusion structure
Well-labelled headings, numbered tables and figures aid navigation
Arguments generally progress sensibly from one step to the next
Material is mostly on-topic and relevant throughout
weaknesses: Some passages remain verbose or digressive (e.g. lengthy historical narrative)
Occasional formatting glitches in tables interrupt flow
Logical leaps (e.g. distance interpretation) could be tightened
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the report has moments of unnecessary narrative and minor formatting issues, it is highly coherent, well organised and flows logically throughout. The clear three-part structure, effective use of headings and smooth progression of ideas justify the top band for Presentation.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (tables, scatter plots, piecewise graphs)
Generally clear mathematical language with correct use of standard symbols
Key equations and regression statistics are well-integrated into the discussion
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation and typographical errors (e.g. misplaced brackets, mixed units)
Low-resolution figures lacking tick labels or gridlines reduce clarity
Some domains and integral limits are omitted or poorly annotated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs a strong variety of mathematical representations and communicates ideas clearly in most places. However, inconsistent notation, unit handling issues and occasional lapses in clarity limit the score to band 3 rather than band 4.

strengths: Clear personal interest in F1 driving the choice of question and data
Creative initiative in combining polynomial, sinusoidal and power functions
Noticeable independent thinking in devising a piecewise regression approach
weaknesses: Voice occasionally lapses into generic textbook commentary
Research decisions remain within taught techniques rather than breaking new ground
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs personal passion for motor-racing visibly shapes the exploration, and the inventive modelling approach shows outstanding engagement and initiative, meriting the top band for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Reflection on findings is meaningful and linked to racing strategy
Limitations are acknowledged and discussed (e.g. video data issues)
Connections made between calculus understanding and realâworld implications
weaknesses: Critical analysis remains superficial and descriptive rather than probing
Over-generalisation in the conclusion without adequate qualifiers
No error or uncertainty analysis on the measurements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers consistent, meaningful reflection and clear connections to their understanding of calculus, but critical analysis is basic and lacks depth, placing this work in band 2 for Reflection.

strengths: Sophisticated use of HL-level calculus: differentiation, integration and piecewise functions
Effective deployment of regression modelling and RÂ² analysis
Demonstrates thorough understanding of mathematical concepts underlying motion analysis
weaknesses: Incomplete distance tables and missing intermediate integration steps
Unit conversions and handling reveal minor inaccuracies
Rationale for model choices (e.g. polynomial vs sinusoid) is not fully justified
Some misinterpretations of results and omitted integral limits
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration makes substantial mathematical demandsâpiecewise HL calculus, regression and integrationâand these are largely executed correctly with only minor errors. The level of sophistication and overall correctness justify band 5 for Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting a personal data source, segmenting the motion, and choosing own modelling strategy show noticeable independent thought, yet decisions remain within taught techniques.  This aligns with significant (not outstanding) evidence, i.e. band 2.

The student shows good conceptual grasp of modelling, integration and differentiation, but misinterpretations of results and unit handling reveal gaps.  Understanding is âgood in partsâ, consistent with band 3.

Several computations (e.g., total distance, integration limits) contain sizeable inaccuracies, yet many derivative calculations and fits are numerically reasonable.  This partial correctness matches band 2.

Most material is on-topic, yet the report is lengthy with repeated data sets, over-long quotations of equations, and unnecessary narrative about F1 history.  Consequently the piece is not consistently concise, placing it in band 2.

Standard symbols (v, t, x, RÂ²) and calculus notation are used, but there are frequent typographical errors (ms-2, misplaced brackets) and mixed units.  Appropriate language is therefore âsomeâ rather than âmostlyâ, matching band 2.

Explanations are generally understandable, but inconsistent notation, abrupt switches between km hâ»Â¹ and m sâ»Â¹, and occasional grammatical confusion reduce overall clarity.  Hence âmostly clear with some consistencyâ (band 2).

Calculus, regression modelling, piecewise functions and RÂ² analysis are all at HL course level and deployed across the task, indicating effective use of relevant mathematics.  The work is not sophisticated enough for bands 5â6, so band 4 (effective) is most fitting.

The student provides tables, scatter plots, piece-wise graphs and regression statistics, allowing the mathematics to be viewed in multiple forms.  Representations support the discussion even if some images are low resolution, so effective use (band 3) is merited.

The student reflects on findings, acknowledges what the results suggest about racing strategy, and discusses limitations.  Reflection is present throughout but remains descriptive rather than probing, hence âmeaningfulâ (band 2).

Combining video analysis with piecewise regression demonstrates creativity and initiative beyond a single straight-line model.  However methods are still relatively standard and the conclusions are conventional.  Band 2 best reflects the level of creativity shown.

The exploration follows a recognisable sequence (introduction â data â modelling â results â conclusion).  Arguments usually progress sensibly from one step to the next, although some passages are verbose or digressive and a few logical leaps (e.g. interpretation of distance) weaken the line of reasoning.  Overall coherence is clear but not exemplary, so band 3 is the best fit.
Critical appraisal of methods is brief and superficial; for example, the validity of huge distance results is not questioned.  Only basic critique is evident, fitting band 1.
The student links reflections to personal understanding of calculus in a racing context, showing a clear though not enhanced connection.  Band 2 is therefore suitable.

Headings, numbered tables and figures give the work a clear three-part structure (introduction/body/conclusion).  Despite formatting glitches in a few tables, the reader can still navigate the report easily.  Transitions between sections are present but not seamless, thus âwell organisedâ (band 3) rather than âexceptionally well organisedâ.

The studentâs interest in F1 clearly shapes the question, data choice and interpretation.  The investigation is therefore driven by a personal approach, but the voice occasionally lapses into generic textbook commentary, so band 2 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The introductory personal narrative shows engagement but veers into lengthy background. Consider condensing historical commentary and focusing more quickly on mathematical aims.
The exploration title is descriptive but could be more precise in reflecting the mathematical focus (e.g. specifying kinematic analysis and calculus methods).
The velocity conversion formula misses unit notation for kmÂ·hâ»Â¹ and misplaces brackets. Write clearly as $$v=\frac{v_{km/h}\times1000}{3600}\;[m/s]$$.
Domains for each function are stated but units of x (seconds) are omitted. Include âsâ in each domain for clarity.
The explanation of RÂ² values should include interpretation of values below 0.9 (e.g. f(x)=0.8095). Reflect on implications for distance integration accuracy.
Conclusion succinctly summarizes findings but over-generalizes implications for race strategy. Add qualifiers on sample size and single-corner focus.
Figure 1 is clearly captioned but lacks commentary on how the circuit shape influences cornering analysis. Add a sentence linking track geometry to mathematical modelling.

The objective restates driver names and season but does not specify a clear research question. Refine to ask, for example, âWhich driverâs cornering yields optimal distance-speed trade-off via calculus?â
In the procedure, RÂ² threshold rationale is mentioned but lacks justification for selecting 0.9. Cite a source or explain why values below this limit are unacceptable.
Table headers under âVelocity to Timeâ and âVelocity m/sâ repeat three times but lack column labels for driver identity. Simplify to two columns and label each table for each driver.
The scatter plots (Figure 10, Figure 11) effectively show fits, yet resolution is low and axes lack tick labels. Improve clarity by enlarging axes and adding gridlines.
The rationale for choosing polynomial vs. sinusoidal segments is not explained. Discuss criteria (e.g. residuals or physical interpretation) used to select each function.
Using piecewise functions shows initiative (combining polynomial, sine, power), demonstrating significant personal creativity.
Table 1 presents seven segments of extremely high-degree polynomials. Consider reducing polynomial degree or combining intervals to avoid overfitting and improve interpretability.
Table 3 domain entries contain stray spacing and OCR glitches ("\[0."). Carefully proofread LaTeX to ensure correct display of limits.
The summation equation for total distance lacks integral limits. Use $$\sum_{i}\int_{t_i}^{t_{i+1}}v_i(x)dx$$ notation to show intervals explicitly.
Distance table omits entries for segments 3â7. Ensure completeness by filling in all integrated values and showing intermediate steps.
Integration results for the sinusoidal segment (54.02 m) seem low relative to other segments. Verify with analytic antiderivative and add numerical check.
Summing distances to claim âfaster paceâ overlooks acceleration and time durations. Add average speed or time per segment analysis for a more rigorous comparison.
Negative acceleration at entry is interpreted as braking, but physical implications (e.g. braking force) are not discussed. Include brief physics context to deepen reflection.
Acceleration comparison lacks error analysis or uncertainty. Discuss possible measurement errors from video telemetry and their impact on derivative values.
Limitations section acknowledges video data but omits discussion on frame-rate quantization. Address how temporal resolution affects integration accuracy.
The bibliography uses inconsistent formatting (e.g. missing italics, inconsistent punctuation). Apply a consistent citation style throughout.
